text:
Our ref.: 11.17.001.009.287
November 26, 2024
Decision
Complaint against CMC Certus Management Consultants Ltd
for unlawful transfer and breach of personal data
With regard to the complaint submitted to my Office on XXXX, by Mr. XXXX
and Mrs. XXXX, hereafter “the Complainants”, through their lawyers, against
CMC Certus Management Consultants Ltd, hereafter “the controller” or “the
company”, through its Director, Mr. XXXX, hereafter “the Director”, in
accordance with Articles 57(1)(f) and 77 of the Regulation (EU) 2016/679,
hereafter the General Data Protection Regulation, “the GDPR”, I issue the
following Decision:
1. Facts of the case
The complaint
1.1. On 15/02/2021, the Complainants entered into a contract with the
company, according to which the latter should provide various services for the
Complainants for obtaining residence permit in Cyprus.
1.2. According to the Complainants, in the context of the contract, they gave to
the Director, among other things, bank account statements, their marriage
certificate and payroll statements with aim to be translated, from German to
Greek.
1.3. At the end of March, beginning of April 2021, the Complainants claimed
they contacted the Director and requested to give them back the documents
they had given him and the translated copies. They also claimed he informed
them that the documents had been sent to Georgia where the company also
maintains offices. The Complainants added they had not been informed about
this transfer and never consented to it.
1.4. On 16/09/2021, the Complainants were informed that their marriage
certificate and Mrs. XXXX payroll statement were posted on the online platform
"Scribd" by a user named “XXXX”, thus being accessible to all users of this
platform. The documents contained the following personal data: full names of
the Complainants, date and place of marriage, date and place of birth, address,
social security number, tax identification number, salary amount for January,
date of employment start and the name of the company with which Mrs XXXX
was insured. The same day, the Complainants contacted the platform and
requested the removal of their documents. The documents were removed the
following day.
1.5. After investigating the incident, the Complainants found out that, the user
named "XXXX" who had uploaded their documents on the platform, could be a
translator employed by CMC LLC, a company based in Georgia.
1.6. On 16/09/2021, the Complainants informed the Director of the controller
about the exposure of their personal data on the online platform "Scribd" and
requested the deletion of all their documents and personal data. They also
requested to be informed, to whom these documents sent to. On 18/09/2021,
the Director replied that the company does not use this platform.
1.7. On 20/09/2021, the Complainants sent an email to the Director of the
controller and attached screenshots, documenting the exposure of their
personal data on the platform. On the same day, the Director replied that the
incident would be investigated and he would reply to them as soon as possible.
Since then, the Complainants did not receive any feedback on this matter.
Correspondence with the Director of the controller
1.8. In the course of the investigation, on 08/04/2022, I informed the Director of
the controller about the complaint and requested his positions. Subsequently,
my Office had extensive email correspondence with him. From his replies dated
11/05/2022, 01/07/2022, 10/05/2023 and 17/07/2023, his positions on the case
are summarized as follows:
(a) The company had entered into an agreement with the Complainants to
offer them consultancy and/or immigration services in Cyprus in order to
obtain a residence permit in Cyprus.
(b) The company requested the Complainants’ bank statements, marriage
certificate and payroll statements because, these documents are
required when submitting residency applications at the Immigration
Office, according to Alien and Immigration Regulations.
(c) The said documents were maintained and/or stored in the Director’s
email account to be used for the residency application and they would
be deleted after the submission of the application.
(d) The documents were translated by a team member of the company’s
office in Larnaca because, according to Alien and Immigration
Regulations, it was not necessary to officially translate them for the
residency application. If the Authorities in Larnaca had requested official
translations, this would be done by a sworn translator.
2
(e) When the Complainants informed the Director that their documents were
exposed on the platform “Scribd”, he replied that his company does not
use this platform, he did not know who uploaded their documents and
that he examined the matter and did not find any breach of his email
account.
(f) When the Complainants requested the deletion of their documents, the
Director immediately complied and deleted them from his email account.
(g) The Director maintained that the documents’ leakage did not occur from
the company’s side and that he could only assume, they leaked during
their transmission from the Complainants to him.
(h) The only nexus between the company and Certus Management
Services LLC, which is based in the Republic of Georgia, is that both
companies are controlled by the Director. The two companies do not
have any connected business activities. The Cypriot company deals with
business consultancy and assists clients to reside in Cyprus. Certus
Management Services LLC mainly deals with clients’ relocation to
Georgia.
(i) Regarding the Complainants’ allegation that he informed them that the
documents had been sent to Georgia for translation, the Director claimed
that he probably mentioned Georgia in another context.
(j) The Director claimed that “XXXX” is a very common name in Georgia
and that he can only assume that it is a coincidence. He added that he
did not know the platform “Scribd” and that, his team members in Cyprus
do not use it either.
(k) The Complainants’ documents were translated by a team member of the
company, at its office in Larnaca.
1.9. In the light of the above, I could not safely conclude that the Complainants'
personal data were breached by the Controller, despite of all the coincidences,
surrounding the case. Therefore, on 19/08/2022, we informed the Director that
the investigation did not produce any evidence supporting a data breach by the
company. However, I cited the following coincidences:
(a) the company in Larnaca and Certus Management Services LLC in the
Republic of Georgia are controlled by the same person, i.e. the Director.
(b) Certus Management Services LLC employees a translator named Mr. XXXX
and the documents in question were uploaded on the platform “Scribd” by a
user named “XXXX”.
1.10. In the same letter, I informed the Director that in lack of supporting
evidence, the investigation was terminated and on 14/09/2022, I informed the
Complainants that in the light of all documentation brought before us, I could
not proceed to further investigation of the complaint.
3
Additional evidence
1.11. On 14/02/2023, the Complainants sent us the following invoices, emails
and messages for proving that their documents were translated in Georgia, not
in Cyprus:
(a) Invoice issued on 19/02/2021, by Certus Management Services LLC, based
in Georgia.
(b) Invoice issued on 24/02/2021, by Certus Management Services LLC, based
in Georgia.
(c) Email sent by the Director to Mr. XXXX on 19/02/2021, attaching the invoice
referred to point (a) above.
(d) Email sent by the Director to Mr. XXXX on 24/02/2021, attaching the invoice
referred to point (b) above.
(e) Mr. XXXX bank statement showing his payment made to Certus
Management Services LLC, in Georgia, for the invoices referred to in points (a)
and (b) above.
(f) Messages exchanged in WhatsApp between 29/03/2021 and 19/04/2021, by
the Director and Mr. XXXX, showing the later inquiring the whereabout of their
documents and the Director replying that he was expecting them to arrive via a
courier.
(g) Email sent by the Director to Mr. XXXX on 29/03/2021, whereby XXXX
mentioning that he would delete all the Complainants’ personal data.
1.12. All of the above were accompanied by translations from German to
English, done by a sworn translator.
Re-opening of the complaint
1.13. In the light of the above, my Office re-opened the case and on 09/05/2023,
I requested the Director’s positions with regard to the new evidence submitted
by the Complainants. On 10/05/2023, the Director of the controller replied that
he could not explain what happened because in conformity with the
Complainants’ request, he had deleted all their personal data and he does not
have any recollection of the case.
1.14. To refresh his recollection of the case, on 16/05/2023 and 29/05/2023, I
sent to the Director all the documents mentioned in paragraph 1.11. above and
all the correspondence he had exchanged with my Office, since 08/04/2022,
respectively. On 17/07/2023, the Director replied that, again, he could not give
any explanations, since he had deleted all his relevant communications with the
Complainants.
4
1.15. It should be noted that on 08/04/2022, before re-opening the case, I asked
the Director for the provisions of Chapter V he had relied upon, for transferring
the said documents to a third country, e.g. the Republic of Georgia. In his reply,
he maintained that no such transfer occurred. He maintained the same position
after the re-opening of the case.
Preliminary decision
1.16. On 25/07/2024, I issued a preliminary decision, concluding a prima facie,
that the transfer of the said documents, from the company in Cyprus, to the
company in the Republic of Georgia, was in violation of Chapter V of the GDPR.
1.17. Also, I concluded a prima facie, that the controller did not take appropriate
technical or organisational measures, for preventing the accidental leakage of
the said documents and their exposure on the platform Scribd, in violation of
Article 5(1)(f) of the GDPR.
1.18. In addition, I concluded that the controller’s failure to provide my Office
with adequate explanations about the facts of the case, a prima facie,
constituted a violation of Article 31 of the GDPR.
1.19. In the context of the right to be heard enshrined in section 43 of Law
158(Ι)/1999, I called the controller to submit to my Office, until 25/08/2024, his
views on (a) the facts of the case, (b) the prima facie violations of the GDPR
cited above, (c) the reasons why I should not impose any sanctions to the
company, and/ or any mitigating factors that I should take into account, before
I deliver my final Decision.
1.20. On 04/09/2024, the controller replied that, he reviewed the documents
very carefully, he had never used Scribed and he first heard about it, in the
context of this case. Also, since he had deleted all documents and
communications with the complainant, as it was demanded of him, he had
difficulty to review the case from his side. In addition, the controller informed
me that they hired a person to deal with compliance and data protection in order
to make sure that they will be always comply with the existing data protection
regulations.
2. Legal framework
2.1. Article 4 of The General Data Protection Regulation, hereafter the “GDPR”,
provides the following definitions:
(1) ‘personal data’ means any information relating to an identified or identifiable
natural person (‘data subject’); an identifiable natural person is one who can be
identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as
a name, an identification number, location data, an online identifier or to one or
more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic,
cultural or social identity of that natural person;
5
(2) ‘processing’ means any operation or set of operations which is performed
on personal data or on sets of personal data, whether or not by automated
means, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage,
adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission,
dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination,
restriction, erasure or destruction;
(7) ‘controller’ means the natural or legal person, public authority, agency or
other body which, alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes and
means of the processing of personal data; where the purposes and means of
such processing are determined by Union or Member State law, the controller
or the specific criteria for its nomination may be provided for by Union or
Member State law;
(12) ‘personal data breach’ means a breach of security leading to the accidental
or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access
to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed;
2.2. The Principle of Integrity and Confidentiality enshrined in GDPR Article
5(1)(f) provides that personal data shall be “processed in a manner that ensures
appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against
unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or
damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures”.
2.3. The Principle of Accountability set out in GDPR Article 5(2) provides that
“The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance
with, paragraph 1.”.
2.4. Article 31 of the GDPR provides that “The controller and the processor and,
where applicable, their representatives, shall cooperate, on request, with the
supervisory authority in the performance of its tasks.”.
2.5. CHAPTER V of the GDPR sets out the conditions for transfers of personal
data to third countries or international organisations. In particular, Article 44 lays
down the general principle for transfers, Article 45 regulates transfers on the
basis of an adequacy decision, Article 46 provides for transfers subject to
appropriate safeguards, including standard contractual clauses, Article 47
enables transfers on the basis binding corporate rules, Article 48 regulates
transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law, Article 49 provides for
derogations for specific situations and Article 50 for International cooperation
for the protection of personal data.
2.6. Article 44 of the GDPR provides that “Any transfer of personal data which
are undergoing processing or are intended for processing after transfer to a
third country or to an international organisation shall take place only if, subject
to the other provisions of this Regulation, the conditions laid down in this
Chapter are complied with by the controller and processor, including for onward
transfers of personal data from the third country or an international organisation
to another third country or to another international organisation. All provisions
in this Chapter shall be applied in order to ensure that the level of protection of
natural persons guaranteed by this Regulation is not undermined.”.
6
2.7. Article 58(2) of the GDPR vests to the Commissioner corrective powers,
which include the following:
(a) to issue warnings to a controller or processor that intended processing
operations are likely to infringe provisions of this Regulation;
(b) to issue reprimands to a controller or a processor where processing
operations have infringed provisions of this Regulation;
(i) to impose an administrative fine pursuant to Article 83, in addition to, or
instead of measures referred to in this paragraph, depending on the
circumstances of each individual case;
(j) to order the suspension of data flows to a recipient in a third country or to an
international organisation.
2.8. The general conditions for imposing administrative fines are laid down in
Article 83.
3. Reasoning
3.1. The core subject matter of the case can be summarized in four questions:
(a) Did the controller transfer the Complainants’ documents, from the company
in Cyprus, to a company in Georgia, also controlled by its Director?
(b) If yes, was this transfer in line with Chapter V of the GDPR?
(c) Did the controller take appropriate technical or organizational measures to
ensure the security of the Complainants’ documents, in line with Article 5(1)(f)
of the GDPR?
(d) Did the controller cooperate with my Office in line with his obligation
stemming from Article 31 of the GDPR?
First question
3.2.1. Based on the evidence brought before me, two invoices were issued for
translation fees. Both invoices were issued by Certus Management Services
LLC, in Georgia, on 19/02/2021 and on 24/02/2021, respectively. However, the
invoices were sent to Mr. XXXX email by the Director in his capacity as the
Director of the controller in Cyprus. One invoice was sent on 19/02/2021 and
the other, on 24/02/2021. From Mr. XXXX bank statement, it is apparent that
the payment for the outstanding invoices was made to Certus Management
Services LLC, in Georgia.
3.2.2. Therefore, as regards first question the answer must be that the evidence
brought before me, provides sufficient proof that the complainants’ documents
were transferred, from the controller in Cyprus, to the company in the Republic
of Georgia.
7
Second question
3.3.1. Chapter V of the GDPR demands that transfers of personal data to third
countries are subject to the conditions and safeguards set out in this Chapter.
Both before and after the re-opening of the case, the Director was asked for the
provisions of Chapter V he had relied upon, for transferring the said documents
to a third country, e.g. the Republic of Georgia. The Director maintained he had
no recollection of the case and could not give any explanation on the matter.
3.3.2. Since it has been established, without any doubt, that the said documents
were transferred from the company in Cyprus to the company in the Republic
of Georgia and in lack of any explanations provided by the Director, before or
after the re-opening of the case, the answer to the second question must be
that this transfer took place in violation of Article 44 of Chapter V of the GDPR.
Third question
3.4.1. Article 5(1)(f) of the GDPR obliges controllers, to have mechanisms in
place for ensuring the security of personal data, including protection against
unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or
damage.
3.4.2. It has been established that the complainants’ documents were uploaded
on the platform Scribd, by a user named XXXX. It has also been established
that the Director’s company in Georgia, employees a person named XXXX.
Furthermore, it has been established that the invoices for the translation of the
documents were issued by the Georgian company and that the payment for
their translation, was made to the Georgian company.
3.4.3. The established findings strongly suggest that the said documents leaked
from the Georgian company. However, it cannot be safely dismissed, that the
documents leaked from the Cypriot company. The Director’s position was that
leakage could have taken place during the transmission of the documents,
when Mr. XXXX emailed them to him. No evidence was brought before me to
support this possibility and the investigation findings, certainly do not advocate
in its favour. In the absence of any evidence suggesting otherwise, from the
facts of the case it can safely be deducted that the documents leaked, either
from the Cypriot company or from the Georgian company.
3.4.4. When the documents were uploaded on Scribd, they were definitely in
the possession of the Cypriot company (the controller). I have no reason to
dispute that the controller did not intentionally upload the said documents on
Scribd. But did the controller prove that they were not uploaded by accident? In
line with Article 5(1)(f), the controller ought to have in place a safety mechanism
for preventing the accidental leakage of documents, either from the company
in Cyprus or from the Georgian company. Moreover, in accordance with the
Principle of Accountability, the controller should have been in a position to
demonstrate that such a mechanism was indeed in place. This Principle lays
the burden of proof onto controllers.
8
3.4.5. In our extensive correspondence, the controller failed to demonstrate that
such a mechanism was in place, for preventing the accidental leakage of the
said documents. If such a mechanism was in place, it was not brought to my
attention. Therefore, the answer to the third question must be that the controller
did not take appropriate technical or organizational measures to ensure the
security of the Complainants’ documents, in line with Article 5(1)(f).
Fourth question
3.5.1. While the Director of the controller replied to all of my Office’s emails in
a timely manner, he failed to provide adequate explanations to certain facts for
the case. For example, he always maintained that the documents were
translated in Cyprus, by a team member of the company’s Larnaca office. When
asked to provide explanation regarding the transfer of the documents, the
Director of the controller responded that he could not provide such explanation,
since he had deleted all relevant communications with the Complainants. The
Director of the controller maintained this view even when he was presented with
the emails, he had sent to Mr. XXXX with the attached invoices issued by the
Georgian company and the proof of payment to this company (our email dated
16/05/2023).
3.5.2. The exposure of his clients’ documents could have a severe impact both
onto his clients and onto the reputation of his companies. Therefore, when he
was informed of this exposure, he should have investigated it more thoroughly
and he should have been in a position to provide more adequate explanations
to my Office, about the facts surrounding this exposure. As a result, the answer
to the fourth question must be that, the controller cooperated with my Office,
but not to the degree and extend demanded of him, by GDPR Article 31.
4. Conclusion
4.1. As regards the second question, taking into account the answer to the first
question cited in paragraph 3.2.2, I conclude that the transfer of the documents,
from the company in Cyprus, to the company in the Republic of Georgia, was
carried out in violation of Article 44 of Chapter V of the GDPR.
4.2. As regards the third question, I conclude that the controller did not take
appropriate technical or organisational measures, for preventing the accidental
leakage of the said documents and their exposure on the platform Scribd, in
violation of Article 5(1)(f) of the GDPR.
4.3. As regards the fourth question, I conclude that the controller failed to
cooperate with my Office, to the degree and extend demanded of him, by GDPR
Article 31.
Mitigating factors
4.4. In my Preliminary Decision, I asked the controller, in the course of his right
to a hearing, to submit to my Office any mitigating factors that should be taken
9
into account, before I deliver my final Decision. He did submit any but having
examined the facts of the case, I considered the following, as mitigating factors:
4.4.1. The Director’s timely response to all our emails.
4.4.2. The incident in question concerned only two data subjects.
4.4.3. The Complainants’ documents were exposed on the platform only for a
short period of time.
4.4.4. The Director deleted the Complainants’ data when asked to do so.
4.4.5. There was no previous complaint against the controller.
4.4.6. No sensitive data have been affected.
4.4.7. The controller employed a person to deal with compliance with existing
data protection rules.
Aggravating factors
4.5. The circumstances of the cases demand that the following aggravating
factors are taken into consideration:
4.5.1. The Director maintained the view that the documents in question were
not transferred to his company in the Republic of Georgia, even after it became
evident that they had been transferred.
4.5.2. As explained in paragraph 3.5.2. in the course of the investigation, the
Director cooperated with my Office, but not to the degree and extend demanded
of him, by GDPR Article 31, since he did not provide more adequate
explanations to my Office, about the facts surrounding this exposure.
Corrective measures
4.6. In view of the above, by virtue of the powers conferred on me by GDPR
Article 58(2)(i) and in accordance with Article 83:
4.6.1. For the transfer of the complainants’ document, from CMC Certus
Management Consultants Ltd in Larnaca, to Certus Management Services LLC
in the Republic of Georgia, in violation of Article 44 of Chapter V of the GDPR,
I impose to CMC Certus Management Consultants Ltd (the controller) an
administrative fine of 2000 euro.
4.6.2. For the absence of appropriate technical or organisational measures, for
preventing the accidental leakage of the said documents and their exposure on
the platform Scribd, in violation of Article 5(1)(f) of the GDPR, I impose to CMC
Certus Management Consultants Ltd (the controller) an administrative fine of
2000 euro.
10
4.7. By virtue of the power conferred on me by GDPR Article 58(2)(b), for not
cooperating with my Office, to the degree and extend demanded by GDPR
Article 31, I issue a Reprimand to CMC Certus Management Consultants Ltd
(the controller).
Irene Loizidou Nicolaidou
Commissioner
For Personal Data Protection
11
